summary: Gruber analyzes the 2004 HP-Apple iPod licensing deal, arguing it represents Apple's transformation of the iPod from a product into a platform. He frames the deal as a massive win for both companies: HP gets a market-leading digital music solution that differentiates it from Dell, while Apple secures long-term platform dominance. Crucially, the deal bypasses Microsoft entirely, denying Redmond its usual strategy of leveraging Windows monopoly into adjacent markets. Gruber draws a careful distinction between Apple's failed 1990s Mac OS cloning and this limited licensing strategy, arguing the iPod can achieve market leadership without monopoly. He dismisses Paul Thurrott's speculation about WMA support as nonsensical, since the entire point of the deal is independence from Microsoft.
key_points: The HP-Apple deal transforms the iPod from a standalone product into a three-pronged digital music platform (iPod/iTunes/iTMS), analogous to how Windows functions as a PC platform.
Limited licensing to premium partners is the correct strategy for the iPod — widespread licensing commoditizes, while selective partnerships preserve brand value and expand market share.
The deal's most significant dimension is that it completely bypasses Microsoft, denying them their usual monopoly-leveraging strategy in the emerging digital media market.
Apple's digital music platform runs on Windows but is independent of it, making Apple a far more appealing partner than Microsoft for companies tired of being commoditized.
The real money in digital music is in hardware (iPods), not software or per-song sales — making the licensing economics compelling for both companies.
money_quotes: The HP deal changes the iPod from product to platform.
Windows-style widespread licensing is for commodity items. Limited licensing is for premium items.
The market leader doesn't have to hold a monopoly.
Anyone who claims Apple could have achieved greater than 50 percent OS market share without sacrificing what makes the Mac the Mac has no comprehension of how Microsoft built the Windows empire.
Apple's music platform runs on Windows, but it is independent of Windows. It is therefore independent of Microsoft.
If they took all your potatoes the last time you invited them to dinner, why invite them again?
They might as well pull down their pants and get out the Vaseline while they're at it.
The surprise isn't that HP said yes, the surprise is that Apple made the offer in the first place.
key_insight: The HP-Apple iPod deal represents Apple's first successful platform licensing strategy — limited, premium, and crucially independent of Microsoft — transforming the iPod from a product into an industry platform.
